## Overview

## Founder CEOs Are Better

### Anneka Gupta

> Working effectively with a founder in founder mode: recognize that the founder has unique power to push initiatives. **Make the founder your ally** — explain what you need and ask them to use their influence. When the founder pushes a pet idea you disagree with, figure out **the objective behind the request** and offer alternative mechanisms to achieve it. "What's the hill I'm going to die on?" — 

^[episodes/lenny/anneka-gupta]

### Annie Pearl

> The origin story: Calendly's founder Tope Awotona personally onboarded the first users, many from his own network. This founder-led sales approach gave them direct feedback on what worked and built early product-market fit conviction before any scaling. The lesson: don't outsource customer discovery until you've done it yourself.

### Carilu Dietrich

> Carilu's contrarian advice: delay hiring your first salesperson as long as possible. Product-led growth and founder-led sales should carry the company through the early stages. When you do hire sales, they should be selling into a market where the product already has proven demand and a clear bottom-up adoption path. Premature sales hires create pressure to build features for enterprise deals befo

### Chip Conley

### Eoghan McCabe

### Jen Abel

### Pete Kazanjy

^[raw/transcripts/lenny/pete-kazanjy.md]

### Shiv Rao

> Shiv shared what he learned from Jensen Huang about founder-led companies. ### Sri Batchu

> Sri has a clear framework for how B2B growth channels should evolve: start with founder-led sales, then hire dedicated salespeople, then begin low-cost targeted marketing (content, community, events), then PR, and only after all of that invest in paid marketing and SEO. ### Wes Chan

> No matter how talented the founder, if the market is small or dying, the company will fail. Wes prioritizes market size and timing over team quality — a great founder in a massive market will always outperform a mediocre founder in a small one. He explicitly does not do market sizing as a top-down exercise; instead he looks for markets that are clearly large based on observable demand.

^[raw/transcripts/20vc/20vc-2022-08-22-20vc-why-market-always-wins-o.md]
